statement: Following the 2012 advisory referendum in which 66.9% supported using the Constitutional Council's draft as the basis for a new constitution, successive Alþingi sessions have failed to enact constitutional reform. The centre-right government of Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson and Bjarni Benediktsson (2013–2016) effectively shelved the draft, arguing it needed further expert review. Subsequent governments (2016–2017, 2017–2021, 2021–2024) introduced partial constitutional bills — notably on natural resources and electoral reform — but none passed into law. The Stjórnarskrárfélagið (Constitutional Society) and other civil society organisations have campaigned to honour the referendum result, framing parliamentary inaction as a democratic deficit. As of early 2026, no provisions of the 2011 draft have been enacted, making it one of the most protracted constitutional reform efforts in modern European democracies.
source_name: Stjórnarskrárfélagið (Constitutional Society of Iceland); Alþingi parliamentary records; Thorvaldur Gylfason, 'Democracy on Ice' (openDemocracy, 2016)
caveats: Whether parliament was obligated to act on an advisory referendum is a matter of political philosophy, not law. Some constitutional scholars argue the draft had technical deficiencies requiring revision. The low turnout (48.9%) weakens the democratic mandate argument, though a two-thirds majority among those who voted is substantial. The issue remains politically divisive.

related_entries.statement: On 20 October 2012, Iceland held a six-question advisory referendum on constitutional reform proposals drafted by the Stjórnlagaráð (Constitutional Council). The first and most significant question — 'Do you wish the proposals of the Constitutional Council to form the basis of a new Constitution?' — received 66.9% yes votes (49,486 yes vs 24,521 no). All six questions received majority yes votes. Turnout was 48.9% (115,890 of approximately 237,000 eligible voters), significantly lower than typical parliamentary election turnout of 80–85%. The referendum was advisory (ráðgefandi), not legally binding, as the Icelandic constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions. Despite the clear majority in favour, no subsequent Alþingi has enacted the draft into law.

The Constitutional Council's 2011 draft constitution includes a natural resources clause (auðlindaákvæðið) stating: 'Iceland's natural resources which are not in private ownership are the common and perpetual property of the nation. No one may acquire the natural resources or their attached rights for ownership or permanent use, and they may never be sold or mortgaged.' The clause envisions a licensing regime with leases limited to 'a modest period of time.' This provision was the subject of Question 2 in the 2012 referendum ('Do you wish that a new Constitution include a provision on national ownership of natural resources, as is in the draft?'), which received 82.9% yes — the highest approval of any of the six questions. The provision is considered economically significant as Iceland's key export sectors (fishing, energy-intensive industry, tourism) rely on natural resources.

Iceland's constitution (Stjórnarskrá lýðveldisins Íslands) contains no explicit provision on transfer of sovereignty to international organisations. Legal scholars have argued that EU accession would require a constitutional amendment under Article 79, which mandates that amendments be passed by the Alþingi, followed by dissolution, a general election, and passage again by the new Alþingi. The 2026 referendum is advisory (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), not binding, as the constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions (Article 26 covers only presidential referral of legislation).

citations.report_title: Treystu þjóðinni — Opið bréf til forsætisráðherra Íslands

claims.claim_text: Í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni 2012 um nýja stjórnarskrá samþykktu yfir 2/3 hlutar kjósenda (67%) að tillögurnar skyldu verða grundvöllur nýrrar stjórnarskrár Íslands.
Alþingi hefur ekki enn virt úrslit þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunnar 2012 um nýja stjórnarskrá.
Stjórnarskrárfélagið telur að ekki eigi að hefja aðildarviðræður við ESB án þess að tryggja rétt þjóðarinnar yfir auðlindum í stjórnarskrá.
